question.text: In which scenario would a reverse cash-and-carry arbitrage be profitable in equity futures?

answer.text: When the traded futures price is below the fair futures price — sell spot (borrow shares), buy futures, and earn the difference
When the traded futures price is above the fair futures price — buy spot and sell futures
When the futures premium equals the cost of carry — no arbitrage, but reverse carry locks in higher returns
When dividend yield is zero and risk-free rate is negative

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Reverse cash-and-carry: when futures are underpriced (traded price < fair price), sell the asset in spot market, invest proceeds at risk-free rate, and buy futures to lock in the repurchase. At expiry, receive the asset via futures delivery. Profit = Fair price - Traded price. **The Trap:** When futures are OVERPRICED (traded > fair), it's normal cash-and-carry (buy spot, sell futures). Reverse applies when futures are underpriced. **Cross-Exam Context:** Series VIII Ch3 covers both cash-and-carry and reverse cash-and-carry for equity. Similar arbitrage mechanics apply in currency (I) and bond (IV) markets. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Reverse cash-and-carry requires the ability to short-sell the underlying — if short-selling is restricted, the arbitrage may not be executable.
